perturbation.original_text: 13.2 Governing Law. This Development Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota, United States of America, disregarding its conflicts of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text:
4.8 Neither Party may make any public announcement concerning this Development Agreement, its subject matter, and the activities and actions it contemplates without the other Party's express written consent.
13.2 Governing Law. This Development Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of France, disregarding its conflicts of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause has been moved to a different section and also been changed to indicate that the contract will be construed under the laws of France. This is a contract between two parties, one in Minnesota and one in France. The original contract stipulated that Minnesota law should govern the contract. Moving the clause to a section on publicity buries the clause, and changing the law can greatly affect the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles and Contractual Certainty
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law from Minnesota to France and burying the clause in an unrelated section undermines the principle of contractual certainty and the parties' reasonable expectations. Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 emphasizes the importance of honoring the parties' choice of law, especially when the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, which Minnesota does as the principal place of business for Cargill. Altering the governing law significantly impacts the interpretation and enforceability of the contract, potentially invalidating clauses that are valid under Minnesota law but not under French law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Minnesota to France and buries the clause in an unrelated section. This undermines the principle of contractual certainty, particularly given that one party's principal place of business is in Minnesota. This directly contradicts the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which emphasizes honoring the parties' choice of law, especially when a state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions connected to a case, where the outcome depends on which law is used. By changing the governing law and obscuring the clause, the contract introduces a conflict of laws issue and makes the enforceability of the agreement questionable.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Indemnification. Bioamber and Cargill agree to waive any and all claims against each other for consequential, punitive, incidental, special, or other forms of "exemplary" losses whether arising in contract, warranty, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise, including any losses relating to lost use, lost profits, lost business, damage to reputation, or lost or diminished financing unless such claims are based on a Party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text:
3. Alternative Feedstock. Bioamber also desires the right to further develop CB1 to utilize cellulosic biomass as the fermentation feedstock. Cargill hereby grants Bioamber the option to modify or convert the Work Plan to include the development or modification of CB1 capable of fermenting such cellulosic feedstock ("Modified Work Plan"). If such option is exercised, (a) the terms and conditions of this Development Agreement shall apply to the Modified Work Plan except (i) new, additional up-front and milestone payments shall apply as provided below, and (ii) any development work performed under the Modified Work Plan will be at a FTE rate which will be equivalent to the FTE rate provided in Section 2.2 above and further adjusted for inflation at the time it goes into effect; and (b) the commercial license attached as Exhibit D shall be expanded to include the resulting strain developed out of the Modified Work Plan with no additional change in the financial terms.

8. Indemnification. Bioamber and Cargill agree to waive any and all claims against each other for incidental losses whether arising in contract, warranty, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise, including any losses relating to lost use, lost profits, lost business, damage to reputation, or lost or diminished financing unless such claims are based on a Party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: The indemnification clause, which outlines the types of damages that each party is liable for, is critical for risk allocation. By moving it into the section about alternative feedstock it is less likely to be read and understood. Also, the clause has been changed to disallow claims for consequential, punitive, special, or other forms of "exemplary" losses. This may not be enforceable in certain jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Limitations on Exclusion of Consequential Damages
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the clause which bars claims for consequential, punitive, special, or other forms of "exemplary" losses could allow one party to sue for these damages. Minnesota Statute § 336.2-719(3) states that limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Moving this clause to a less visible location, combined with the removal of important conditions, increases the risk of a court finding the altered clause unenforceable, especially if it is deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-719 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-719 336.2-718 336.2-720 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-719 Version List 336.2-719 CONTRACTUAL MODIFICATION OR LIMITATION OF REMEDY. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this chapter. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. History: 1965 c 811 s 336 .2-719 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause barring claims for consequential, punitive, special, or other forms of 'exemplary' losses. Minnesota Statute § 336.2-719(3), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses the limitation of consequential damages. It states that while limitations on consequential damages for personal injury in consumer goods are 'prima facie unconscionable,' limitations on damages where the loss is commercial are not. By removing the original clause, the contract now potentially allows claims for consequential damages that were previously waived, and this removal could be problematic under Minnesota law, especially if the court finds the alteration unconscionable, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 13.8 Entire Agreement. This Development Agreement supersedes all previous understandings between Cargill and Bioamber concerning the subject matter of this Development Agreement, including but not limited to the Term Sheet executed by the Parties on December 3, 2009 and December 4, 2009, and the Mutual Confidentiality Agreement between the Parties which was effective July 17, 2009, and, together with its attachments, including the Commercial License Agreement attached hereto as Exhibit D executed concurrently with this Development Agreement, contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and may not be amended, modified, or supplemented except in writing and signed by both Parties specifically referring to this Development Agreement and the Commercial License Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.1 Each party shall retain ownership of all intellectual property that it owned prior to the Effective Date.
13.8 Entire Agreement. This Development Agreement supersedes all previous understandings between Cargill and Bioamber concerning the subject matter of this Development Agreement, including but not limited to the Term Sheet executed by the Parties on December 3, 2009 and December 4, 2009, and the Mutual Confidentiality Agreement between the Parties which was effective July 17, 2009, and, together with its attachments, including the Commercial License Agreement attached hereto as Exhibit D executed concurrently with this Development Agreement, contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and may not be amended, modified, or supplemented except in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "specifically referring to this Development Agreement and the Commercial License Agreement", any written document signed by both parties could be construed to amend or modify the agreement. Moving this clause to section 5.1 Intellectual Property is problematic because it is not easily recognizable as an important legal provision and could be overlooked. This can be considered predatory and would be looked upon unfavorably.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Parol Evidence Rule and Contract Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the phrase 'specifically referring to this Development Agreement and the Commercial License Agreement' from the integration clause weakens the protection provided by the parol evidence rule under UCC § 2-202. This rule generally prevents the introduction of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict a fully integrated written contract. By eliminating the requirement for amendments to specifically reference the Development Agreement and Commercial License Agreement, the altered clause allows for the potential admission of extrinsic evidence to modify or supplement the terms of the agreement, contradicting the intent of a fully integrated contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract required amendments to specifically reference the Development Agreement and Commercial License Agreement. By removing this requirement, the changed text allows for modifications or supplements to the agreement through other means, such as prior agreements. UCC § 2-202, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a final written expression of an agreement may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement. The changed text weakens the 'entire agreement' clause, undermining the protection against extrinsic evidence contradicting the written contract, and therefore creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction.
